coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection alkaline phosphatase activity using 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qing
Li, Hongxia
Jin, Rui
Li, Ning
Yan, Xu
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a fluorescent bi-enzyme sensing platform for ultrasensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity was developed based on 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoclusters (APBA-Au NCs). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) can catalyze hydrolysis of phenyl phosphate to phenol, which can be subsequently hydroxylated to generate catechol in the presence of tyrosinase (TYR). Due to the special covalent combination between the catechol and boric acid group, the five-membered cyclic esters can be formed on the ligands of APBA-Au NCs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of the Au NCs. Thus, according to the change of fluorescence signal, the proposed biosensor can be utilized to ultrasensitive detection ALP. Under the optimal condition, there exhibited good linear relationships between fluorescence intensity change of APBA-Au NCs and the concentration of ALP in the range of 0.02–2.0 U/L. The detection limits reached 0.005 U/L. The developed strategy was employed to monitor enzyme activity in real samples with satisfactory results obtained, illustrating the potential application of this biosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Functionalization
Ultrasensitive
Alkaline phosphatase

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575048 and No. 21775052 ), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH ). X. Yan is thankful for support from the National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents ( BX201700096 ) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project No. 2017M621199.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic representation for fabrication of APBA functionalized Au NCs; (B) Schematic illustration of sensing strategy for monitoring ALP activity based on APBA-Au NCs.

(A) The TEM image of GSH-Au NCs. Inset shows the size distribution of GSH-Au NCs. (B) The TEM image of APBA-Au NCs. Inset shows the size distribution of APBA-Au NCs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of GSH (black line), GSH-Au NCs (red line) and APBA-Au NCs (blue line). (D) The zeta potential of APBA-Au NCs and Au NCs. (E). EIS of bare GCE, Au NCs/GCE and APBA-Au NCs/GCE in 0.1 mol/L KCl solution containing 2.5 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. The impedance spectra were recorded in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz-100 kHz with the amplitude of 5 mV. (F) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GSH-Au NCs (black line), APBA-Au NCs (red line), APBA (blue line) and the fluorescence emission spectrum of GSH-Au NCs (green line) and APBA-Au NCs (pink line) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of a: APBA-Au NCs; b: APBA-Au NCs + TYR; c: APBA-Au NCs + phenol; d: APBA-Au NCs + ALP; e: APBA-Au NCs + PP; f: APBA-Au NCs + TYR + phenol; g: APBA-Au NCs + TYR + phenol + PP; h: APBA-Au NCs + TYR + phenol + ALP; i: APBA-Au NCs + TYR + PP + ALP. Concentration: TYR, 5.0 U/mL; phenol, 20.0 μmol/L. ALP, 5.0 U/ L; PP, 100 μmol/L. (B) The fluorescence spectra of APBA-Au NCs (black line) and APBA-Au NCs + TYR + phenol (red line). Insets show the photographs of a: APBA-Au NCs; b: APBA-Au NCs + TYR + phenol under the UV light. Concentration: TYR, 5.0 U/mL; phenol, 20.0 μmmol/L. (C) The fluorescence spectra of GSH-Au NCs (black line) and GSH-Au NCs + TYR + phenol (red line). Concentration: TYR, 5.0 U/mL; phenol, 20.0 μmmol/L. (D) The absorption spectrum of TYR (black line), phenol (red line), APBA (blue line), TYR + phenol (pink line), TYR + phenol + APBA (green line) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of APBA-Au NCs/phenol system in the presence of various concentrations of TYR (from up to bottom were 0.0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 6.0, 8.0 U/mL, respectively). (B) The linearity of the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) versus TYR concentration. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of APBA-Au NCs/phenol system in the absence and presence of TYR, respectively.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of APBA-Au NCs/PP/TYR system in the presence of various concentrations of ALP (from up to bottom were 0.0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 U/L, respectively). (B) The linearity of the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) versus ALP concentration. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of APBA-Au NCs/PP/TYR system in the absence and presence of ALP, respectively. (C) The selectivity of the APBA-Au NCs/PP/TYR system for detection of ALP. (D) The FL intensity of the APBA-Au NCs/PP/ALP//TYR system with the interfering substances. (5 U/mL ALP; 10 μg/mL TYR; 100 μg/mL Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, G; 50 μg/mL alanine, histidine, threonine, glycine, glutamate, pepsin, urease, trypsin, GOx, BSA).
Comparison of different fluorescence methods for the determination of ALP.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive detection alkaline phosphatase activity using 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electron Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electron Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Respiratory, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun, 130031, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of sensing strategy for monitoring ALP activity based on 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoclusters.

Herein, a fluorescent bi-enzyme sensing platform for ultrasensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity was developed based on 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoclusters (APBA-Au NCs). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) can catalyze hydrolysis of phenyl phosphate to phenol, which can be subsequently hydroxylated to generate catechol in the presence of tyrosinase (TYR). Due to the special covalent combination between the catechol and boric acid group, the five-membered cyclic esters can be formed on the ligands of APBA-Au NCs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of the Au NCs. Thus, according to the change of fluorescence signal, the proposed biosensor can be utilized to ultrasensitive detection ALP. Under the optimal condition, there exhibited good linear relationships between fluorescence intensity change of APBA-Au NCs and the concentration of ALP in the range of 0.02–2.0 U/L. The detection limits reached 0.005 U/L. The developed strategy was employed to monitor enzyme activity in real samples with satisfactory results obtained, illustrating the potential application of this biosensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Biological enzyme, along with metabolic pathway(s), control a variety of physiological processes in an organism [1]. Maladjustment of enzymes activities are mostly contributed to translational genetic mutations, transcriptional or translational errors, or post-translational modiﬁcation, translation or degradation [2]. Alkaline phosphatases (ALP) as a membrane-bound enzyme, which can catalyze the dephosphorylation process of proteins, nucleic acids, and small molecules, is widely present in the tissues of living organisms [3]. As an important biomarker in medical diagnosis, abnormal levels of ALP activity in serum may reﬂect several diseases, such as liver dysfunction or damage, bone disease, and prostatic cancer, etc. [4,5]. Up to date, a variety of techniques have been reported for ALP activity detection, including electrochemistry [6], chemiluminescence [7], surface-enhanced resonance Raman scattering (SERRS) [8] and colorimetry [9,10]. However, laborious steps of washing and modifications of electrodes for electrochemistry, and professional technology and sophisticated spectrometer for SERRS, such disadvantages made these strategies not appropriate for application in biological systems [11,12]. Colorimetric sensing of ALP based on aggregation of gold and silver nanoparticles can be easily influenced by chemicals, such as amino acids containing sulfhydryl, salt and cyanide, and have a limitation of cross-reactivity with similar kind of analytes [13]. Recently, fluorescence-based methods have sparked signiﬁcant excitement owing to their simplicity, high sensitivity, cost eﬀ ;ectiveness and real time detection. Several ﬂuorescent assays for ALP have been developed by using organic molecules [14,15], ﬂuorescent polymers [16,17], and inorganic quantum dots (QDs) [18,19]. However, these probes have suffered from some of their own disadvantages, for instance, poor solubility in aqueous systems for organic dyes, complicated synthesis process for fluorescent polymers, and some toxicity for QDs [20,21]. Furthermore, many of these biosensors have been found not sensitive enough to monitor ALP levels in human serum, so their application in bioassays is restricted [22]. Thus, the development of a highly sensitive, selectivity biosensor for the determination of ALP is still a great challenge.

Fluorescent metal nanoclusters (NCs) as promising fluorescent nanomaterials for the biomedical applications have sparked wide-spread interest owing to their unusual physicochemical properties and good optical properties [23,24]. The size of metal NCs is smaller than 2 nm between metal atoms and plasmonic metal nanoparticles [25]. Unlike large nanoparticles, ultra-small fluorescent NCs do not display surface plasmon resonance absorption in the visible region but exhibit apparent fluorescence emission in the ultraviolet (UV) to near infrared region [26,27]. Especially, fluorescent gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) have attracted much more attention for sensing and bioimaging, which can be attributed to their incomparable advantages, such as facile easily preparation, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility and facile functionalized with other biomolecules [28–30].

In this paper, we constructed an optical bi-enzyme sensing platform for ALP detection based on the 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoclusters (APBA-Au NCs). The detection strategy was shown in Scheme 1 . APBA was covalently linked to the glutathione-capped Au NCs by means of the carboxyl group of Au NCs reacting with the amino group of APBA through the amide bond. Phenyl phosphate (PP) can be hydrolyzed to produce phenol in presence of ALP under the alkaline condition. Based on the specific reaction between phenylboronic acid and catechol (the hydroxylation products of phenol by TYR), which can form five-membered cyclic esters, so the FL intensity of APBA-Au NCs could be quenched. The integration of bi-enzyme specificity and boronic acid-assisted specific recognition not only improves the sensitivity and selectivity of the biosensing platform but also provides a new way for probing other phenol product-related enzyme system.

Glutathione (GSH), Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA), phenol, phenyl phosphate (PP), 1-ethyl-3-[3-di-methylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and amino acid were purchased from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Tyrosinase (TYR), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), urease, trypsin, pepsin, and glucose oxidase (GOx) were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. Other inorganic reagents (analytically pure) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Co. and were used without further purification. The water used in all experiments had a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm−1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained using a Hitachi H-800 electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV with a CCD camera. FT-IR spectra were performed by a 16 Bruker IFS66 V FT-IR spectrometer equipped with a DGTS detector (16 scans). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with an UV-1700 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan) Fluorescence spectra were measured on F-2700 spectrofluorophotometer (HITACHI Co., Ltd., Japan). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis was performed on a CHI 660B electrochemical workstation in 2.5 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] with 0.1 mol/L KCl as the supporting electrolyte. All pH measurements were made with a PHS-3C pH meter (Tuopu Co., Hangzhou, China). All temperature measurements were carried out using a water bath. All the optical measurements were accomplished at room temperature.

The GSH-Au NCs were prepared according to the previously reported method [31]. The aqueous solutions of GSH (100 mmol/L, 0.3 mL) was added to HAuCl4 (20 mmol/L, 1.0 mL) and 8.7 mL ultrapure water (resistivity > 18 MΩ cm−1). The mixture was stirred at 25 ℃ for 5 min. After that, the temperature was heated to 70 ℃ and kept for 24 h. The light-yellow Au clusters were obtained. Then, the resulting Au NCs solutions were puriﬁed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The resultant GSH-Au NCs were stored at 4 °C.

2 mL GSH-Au NCs solution was mixed with EDC (0.0116 g). After incubated for 1 h at room temperature, APBA (0.0041 g) was added to the system and the reaction was stirred for another 2 h. Finally, the obtained APBA-Au NCs was dialyzed by a 1000 Da MWCO dialysis bag under stirring for 6 h.

For the detection of ALP, 10 μL PP (10 mmol/L), 30 μL pH 8.2 Tris−HCl (10 mmol/L) and 10 μL different concentrations of ALP was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. After that, 905 μL pH 7.8 Tris−HCl (10 mmol/L), 5 μL TYR (1000 U/mL) and 40 μL APBA-Au NCs was added to the mixtures to give final volumes of 1.0 mL. After incubating at 37 °C for another 20 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded from 450 nm to 700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm.

The drug-free blood samples, collected from the Changchun China Japan Union Hospital, were centrifuged at 10000 rpm (6820 g) for 10 min at room temperature. Then, the supernatant was diluted to a 10-fold dilution with deionized water, and different concentrations of ALP were added to the serum samples to prepare the spiked samples. For ALP assays in real sample, the operation steps are the same as those of the part 2.3, only replace the corresponding enzyme by spiked serum sample.

The APBA-Au NCs were synthesized by the EDC condensation reaction of the carboxyl group of GSH-Au NCs and the amino group of APBA (Scheme 1A). Various methods were employed for characterization of APBA-Au NCs. Fig. 1 A and B showed the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Au NCs and APBA-Au NCs. It can be seen that both the Au NCs and APBA-Au NCs are near spherical with well-dispersion, and the average diameter of APBA-Au NCs (1.53 ± 0.42 nm) is larger than that of Au NCs (1.44 ± 0.40 nm), which may be attributed to capping agents densely packed on the Au NCs [32]. FT-IR spectrometry was displayed to study the existence of surface functional groups on the NCs. As shown in Fig. 1C, the characteristic stretching band for SH (2526 cm−1) in GSH disappeared from the surface of Au NCs and APBA-Au NCs, indicating the formation of stable Au NCs through covalent bonds between Au and thiol group. After surfactant encapsulation with APBA, new peaks at 1521 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 were exhibited in APBA-Au NCs spectrum, which are corresponding to the characteristic band of -B(OH)2 from APBA. This could confirm that APBA was successfully linked to the ligands of Au NCs [32,33]. Moreover, from Fig. 1D, after the conjugation of APBA onto Au NCs, the zeta potential of APBA-Au NCs is −22.01 mV, which is higher than that of Au NCs (−29.30 mV). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to demonstrate the modification procedures, as shown in Fig. 1E. The EIS of the bare GCE displayed an almost straight line, which was characteristic of a mass diffusion limiting process. The semicircle diameter at higher frequencies was attributed to the electron-transfer resistance (Ret), and the linear part at lower frequencies was corresponding to the diffusion process. It was observed that the electron-transfer resistance of APBA-Au NCs had a slight increase than that of Au NCs, which suggested the hindrance effect of the APBA to the electrical conductivity. Both the Au NCs and APBA-Au NCs showed a low electron-transfer resistance illustrating that gold nanoclusters have good electrical conductivity. As shown in Fig. 1F, the absorption spectrum of APBA exhibited two characteristic sharps peak at 295 nm and 234 nm, which were probably due to π−π* and n−π* electronic transition, respectively. The absorption spectrum of APBA-Au NCs around 290 nm and 250 nm displayed distinct difference from that of Au NCs, which were attributed to the presence of APBA on the surface of nanomaterial. The attachment of APBA onto Au NCs also lead to the red shift 5 nm of emission peak. All these results demonstrated the Au NCs were successfully capped by APBA.

The designed sensing strategy for fluorescence monitoring of ALP is depicted in Scheme 1. Phenyl phosphate (PP) could be catalyzed by ALP to generate phenol. In the presence of TYR, phenol was oxidized into catechol, which could covalently combine with surface ligands of APBA-Au NCs to form stable five-membered cyclic boronate esters. In the present case, the non-radiative photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process and the partial conﬁguration change are likely to occur between Au NCs and the five-membered cyclic boronate esters [34–37]. This non-radiative recombination was attributed to the introduction of trap states (five-membered cyclic boronate esters) as quencher in APBA-Au NCs, which lead to the fluorescence quenching of Au NCs [38,39]. So, this sensing platform can also be applied for the determination of ALP.

Some experiments were carried out to confirm the feasibility of our sensing system. As shown in Fig. 2 A, alone TYR, phenol, PP and ALP did not lead to an obvious alteration in the FL intensity of APBA-Au NCs. While phenol/TYR or PP/ALP/TYR can obviously quench the fluorescence signal of APBA-Au NCs. In addition, with the same concentration of phenol and TYR, the FL intensity of APBA-Au NCs can be quenched 60% and corresponding fluorescent photographs under the UV light were shown in insets (Fig. 2B), while the FL intensity of Au NCs can only be quenched 15% (Fig. 2C). This demonstrated that our sensing method with APBA-Au NCs possessed higher sensitivity. Moreover, there was a negligible influence on the APBA-Au NCs/TYR/phenol system in the presence of corresponding concentration of ALP and PP. From UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2D), the spectrum of phenol showed two major characteristic absorption peaks at 270 nm and 218 nm. With TYR addition, phenol could be catalyzed to quinine, which appeared a new absorption at 280 nm. When TYR, phenol and APBA mixed together, the absorption spectrum showed two absorption peaks at 318 nm and 513 nm. Neither of them was ascribed for the APBA or phenol. In addition, FT-IR spectra were applied to further investigate the formation of the complex. As shown in Fig. S1, a new peak at 1074 cm−1 appeared in APBA-Au NCs/phenol/TYR system, which was attributed to the characteristic band of borate ester, and there was no similar peak in APBA-Au NCs spectrum. Therefore, we can confirm that APBA could reactive toward catechol to form stable boronate esters complexes.

Because the phenol can be produced by ALP enzymatic hydrolysis of PP, we can exploit APBA-Au NCs/TYR sensing system for screening and detection of ALP. Thus, to gain the best sensitivity of this sensing platform for the detection of ALP, the effect of TYR concentrations on the sensing system was systematically studied. TYR is a kind of oxygenase, which can selectively hydroxylated phenol to yield catechol. In this work, we construct a sensing platform based on the specific recognition between APBA and intermediate product catechol. And, the extent of an enzyme reaction is mainly related to the amount of enzyme substrate, reaction time and environment. So, with sufficient TYR, we firstly optimize the amount of substrate (phenol). As shown in Fig. S2, the FL intensity of APBA-Au NCs dramatically decreased with the increasing of phenol concentration in the range of 0–20.0 μmol/L. When phenol concentration exceeded 20.0 μmol/L, the FL intensity decrease was obviously reduced. In addition, we also studied the interaction between Au NCs and phenol. From Fig. S3, it can be found that the quenching effect of phenol on Au NCs was gentle in the range of 0–20.0 μmol/L, confirming that APBA can improve the sensitivity. From Figs. S4 and S5, it could be observed that maximum quenching degree was obtained at pH 7.8, and the optimal reaction time was 20 min. Under the optimal conditions, different concentration of TYR was introduced to the sensing system. From Fig. 3 A, it can be seen that with the increasing of TYR, the FL intensity of Au NCs was gradually quenched. The FL intensity ratio (F/F0) and enzyme concentration exhibited good linear relationships (R2>0.99, Fig. 3B). The detection limit of TYR is 0.021 U/mL. So, this could provide an approach for the determination of TYR based on the specific recognition between APBA and intermediate product catechol.

To evaluate its performance for detection a specific species in a complex biomedical system, selectivity is one of the essential parameters of developed sensing platform. So, various potential interfering substances, including inorganic salts (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+), amino acids (alanine, histidine, arginine, threonine, glycine, glutamate) and other biological molecules such as glucose, pepsin, urease, trypsin, GOx, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and ALP were respectively introduced into sensing system. As shown in Fig. 4C, the FL intensity of APBA-Au NCs/PP/TYR system did not exhibit a remarkable decrease after mixed with any of the compounds except ALP. The high selectivity of biosensor was owing to the special affinity of boronic acid group and the specificity catalyzation of enzyme. Interference experiments were carried out and the results were displayed in the Fig. 4D. Notably, the same fluorescence response to ALP was obtained before and after introduction above-mentioned substances into APBA-Au NCs/PP/TYR/ALP system. All above results illustrate that the developed sensing system possesses excellent selectivity and good anti-interference capability for ALP determination.

The normal level of ALP in human serum is in a range of 40–150 U/L for adults [16,39]. Our method was further applied for ALP determination in human serum samples. ALP were all found in three serum samples collected from healthy volunteers, which were in accordance with the report of the hospital. Then, different levels of standard ALP were added to the three serum samples for detection by the proposed method. The standard addition results are listed in Table S1, it can be seen that the average recoveries of ALP were in the range of 92.6–105.3% and the RSD (n = 3) was lower than 4.0%. These results suggested that the developed method can be applied to ALP detection in clinical diagnosis.

In summary, we constructed a fluorescence sensing platform for ALP detection based on APBA-Au NCs specific recognition of hydroxylation products of phenol by TYR, which can form boronate esters, and then quench the fluorescence signal of APBA-Au NCs. Also, phenol could be produced by phenyl phosphate via the ALP-catalyzed reaction. By means of bi-enzyme specific catalysis and specific recognition of phenylboronic acid, excellent selectivity and sensitivity were obtained. 0.005 U/L of ALP detection limit is much lower than most of the previous sensing methods. The satisfactory results of real samples assay suggest that the proposed APBA-Au NCs is expected to be a good fluorescence material for selective enzyme detection in the biological samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575048 and No. 21775052), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH). X. Yan is thankful for support from the National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX201700096) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project No. 2017M621199.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.083.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzyme and metabolic engineering for the production of novel biopolymers: crossover of biological and chemical processes
Measuring enzyme activity in single cells
A fluorometric assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots through host-guest recognition
Elevation of serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) level in postmenopausal women is caused by high bone turnover
High-molecular intestinal alkaline phosphatase in chronic liver diseases
Ratiometric electrochemical detection of alkaline phosphatase
Horseradish peroxidase sol-gel immobilized for chemiluminescence measurements of alkaline-phosphatase activity
Detection of alkaline phosphatase using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy
Nucleic acid-regulated perylene probe-induced gold nanoparticle aggregation: a new strategy for colorimetric sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity and inhibitor screening
Sensing phosphatase activity by using gold nanoparticles
Facile and sensitive near-infrared fluorescence probe for the detection of endogenous alkaline phosphatase activity in vivo
MnO2 nanosheet-carbon dots sensing platform for sensitive detection of organophosphorus pesticides
Fast and sensitive near-infrared fluorescent probes for ALP detection and 3d printed calcium phosphate scaffold imaging in vivo
Protein stabilized fluorescent gold nanocubes as selective probe for alkaline phosphatase via inner filter effect
A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on ESIPT and AIE processes for alkaline phosphatase activity assay and visualization in living cells
Ratiometric fluorescent probe for alkaline phosphatase based on betaine-modified polyethylenimine via excimer/monomer conversion
A label-free conjugated polymer-based fluorescence assay for the determination of adenosine triphosphate and alkaline phosphatase
Fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with β-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots
A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots
Sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase by dephosphorylation-initiated transcription reaction-mediated dual signal amplification
FRET effect between fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles and MnO2 nanosheets and its application for sensitive sensing of alkaline phosphatase
A turn-on fluorescent sensor for selective and sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with gold nanoclusters based on inner filter effect
Recent advances in biomedical applications of fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Ultra-small fluorescent metal nanoclusters: synthesis and biological applications
Fluorescent metal nanoclusters: From synthesis to applications
Fluorescent gold nanoclusters: recent advances in sensing and imaging
Water-soluble gold nanoclusters prepared by protein-ligand interaction as fluorescent probe for real-time assay of pyrophosphatase activity
Gold nanoparticle-based photoluminescent nanoswitch controlled by host-guest recognition and enzymatic hydrolysis for arginase activity assay
An ammonia-based etchant for attaining copper nanoclusters with green fluorescence emission
From aggregation-induced emission of Au(I)-thiolate complexes to ultrabright Au(0)@Au(I)-thiolate core-shell nanoclusters
Photostability of the oleic acid-encapsulated water-soluble CdxSeyZn1–xS1–y gradient core-shell quantum dots
A reusable interface constructed by 3-aminophenylboronic acid-functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes for cell capture, release, and cytosensing
Facile synthesis of red emitting 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized copper nanoclusters for rapid, selective and highly sensitive detection of glycoproteins
A fluorometric biosensor based on functional Au/Ag nanoclusters for real-time monitoring of tyrosinase activity
Fluoride anion sensing mechanism of 2-ureido-4[1H]-pyrimidinone quadruple hydrogen-bonded supramolecular assembly: photoinduced electron transfer and partial configuration change
Sensing mechanism for a fluoride chemosensor: invalidity of excited-state proton transfer mechanism
Surface plasmon inhibited photo-luminescence activation in CdSe/ZnS core-shell quantum dots
Photo-stability of CsPbBr3 perovskite quantum dots for optoelectronic application
Inhibition of dsDNA-templated copper nanoparticles by pyrophosphate as a label-free fluorescent strategy for alkaline phosphatase assay
Label-free upconversion nanoparticles-based fluorescent probes for sequential sensing of Cu2+, pyrophosphate and alkaline phosphatase activity
Fluorescence immunoassay based on the phosphate-triggered fluorescence turn-on detection of alkaline phosphatase
Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate
Fluorescent light-up probe with aggregation-induced emission characteristics for alkaline phosphatase sensing and activity study
A fluorescent turn-on probe for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity in living cells
Reaction-based off-on near-infrared fluorescent probe for imaging alkaline phosphatase activity in living cells and mice
Electrochemically generated green-fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots for facile monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity based on the Fe3+-mediating ON-OFF-ON-OFF fluorescence principle
A luminescent europium-dipicolinic acid nanohybrid for the rapid and selective sensing of pyrophosphate and alkaline phosphatase activity

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qing Liu received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 2016. Now, she is currently carrying out her graduate work for her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Prof. Xingguang Su in Jilin University. Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis and functionalization of metal nanoclusters and their application on biological enzyme detection.

Hongxia Li received her M.S. degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University and received her Ph.D. degree in 2016 at Jilin University. Since then, she did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu. Currently, her research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Rui Jin received her M.S. degree in 2016 from College of Chemistry, Jilin University, China. Currently, she is studying for her Ph.D. degree in College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University. Her research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Ning Li is currently carrying out her graduate work for her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Prof. Xingguang Su in Jilin University. Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis and functionalization of nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Xu Yan received his M.S degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University. He joined the group of Prof. Xingguang Su at Jilin University and received his Ph.D. degree in June 2017. Since then, he did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/biosensors.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her PhD degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
